P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
Technical Assistance 
(916) 322·5662 
Administration 
Executive/legal 
322·5901 
Enforcement 
322-6441 
322·5660 
FredericK K. Lowell 
Pillsbury, Madison & Sutro 
225 Bush Street 
P.O. Box 7880 
San Francisco, CA 
94120 
Dear Mr. Lowell: 
February 7, 1983 
Re: 
Our File No. A-83-012 
Stateme n ts of Econ om i c Interest 
322-6444 
In your letter of January 18, 1983, you asked a number of 
questions concerning the scope of Government Code Section 84308* 
("AB 1040") and the Commission's regulations interpreting that 
section. 
In particular, you asked about: 
(1) 
The definition of "agent" in 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 
18438.3(b) i 
(2) 
The inclusion of contributions to federal election 
campaigns in the definition of contrioutions in 2 Cal. Adm. Code 
Section 18438.5 (a) i 
(3) 
The provision for aggregation of contrioutions in 
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18438.5(b) i 
(4) 
The applicability of Section 84308 to a situation in 
which a partner of an individual, or a PAC or other memOers of a 
trade association to which an individual oelongs, make 
contributions to memoers of a quasi-judicial board or commission 
before which tne individual has an application pending. 
Because you did not aSK these questions on behalf of 
specifically identified clients, and did not provide a 
I 
*All statutory references are to the Government Code unless 
otherwise noted. 


Frederick K. Lowell 
February 7, 1983 
Page 2 
.pecific factual context for the questions, this letter does not 
constitute advice under Section 83114. 
See 2 Cal. Adm. Code 
Section 18329 (b). 
However, 'I will try to answer your questions 
in general terms in order to clarify the Commission's intent in 
adopting its regulations, and to point out areas in which you 
may wish to make requests for more specific advice under Section 
83114 on behalf of your clients. 
1. 
Definition of Agent: 
You have asKed whether an 
architectural, engineering or law firm that "merely" provides 
technical data which is included in an application is the 
applicant's "agent" within the meaning of Section 84308 and 
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18438.3(b). 
In general, unless such a 
firm eitner appears before tne board or commission in person, or 
communicates directly with the members of the board or commis-
sion, we would not consider the firm to be an agent. 
However, 
when a firm goes beyond "mere provision of technical data" --
for example, when an engineering firm prepares an application" 
including written materials bearing the engineering firm's name 
and advocating the applicant's position -- the firm might be 
considered to be "directly communicating" with the board or 
commission and thus to be an "agent" of the applicant. 
Clearly, 
there will be many gradations of involvement by such firms in 
applications to agencies covered by Section 84308. 
When you are 
faced with a specific situation for a specific client, we will 
be glad to provide you with more specific advice. 
2. 
Definition of Contributions: 
In your second question 
you asked about 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18438.5(a) , which 
defines contributions for purposes of Section 84308. 
You 
suggested that the inclusion of federal contributions in that 
definition is preempted by 2 USC Section 453. 
In this respect I 
suggest that you look at the Opinion of Counsel of the Federal 
Elections Commission (OC 1975-14) concerning preemption of a 
Massachusetts statute containing provisions similar to Section 
84308. 
I have included a copy of the opinion for your con-
venience. 
The opinion concluded that a number of provisions 
relating to contributions, including restrictions on the 
solicitation and receipt of campaign contributions by appointed 
state officials (Chapter 55, Mass. General Laws, Sections 
11-17), were not preempted by 2 USc Section 453. 
In light of 
this opinion it does not appear that there is any preemption 
problem with the definition of contribution in 2 Cal. Adm. Code 
Section 18438.5(a} i therefore there is no need to consider the 
application of Article III, Section 3.5 of the California 
Constitution to Section 84308 or to the Commission's regulation. 


Frederick K. Lowell 
February 7, 1983 
Page 3 
You also asked whether the definition in 2 Cal. Adm. Code 
Section 18438.5(a) applies to Doth Section 84308(a) and Section 
84308(0). 
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18438.5 (a) was intended to 
provide a single definition of contribution for Section 84308, 
and thus applies to Secton 84308(a) as well as to Section 
84308 (b) . 
3. 
Aggregation of Contrioutions: 
You raised two questions 
regarding 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18438.5(b). 
First, you point 
out that the statute refers to contributions from an applicant 
or his or her agent, and question whether, in view of this 
language, the statute permits any aggregation of contributions. 
The word "or," while most often used as a disjunctive term, is 
often used in stautes as a conjunctive term. 
See, e.g. De Sylva 
v. Ballentine (1956) 76 S.Ct. 974, 976, 977, 351 US 570, 573, 
575, 1002 Ed 1415. 
In fact, there are a number of other places 
in the Political Reform Act where "or" is used in a conjunctive 
sense. 
E.g. Section 84200(b) 
(" ... in an odd-numbered year, 
have made contributions or independent expenditures of five 
h undr ed dollar s ... ") i Section 87103 (a) 
("any bus iness en ti ty in 
which the public official has a direct or indirect interest 
worth more than one thousand dollars .. :") . 
The Commission has determined that the interpretation in 
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18438.5(b) best carries out the 
purposes of Section 84308. 
It would defeat the purposes of this 
section if an applicant and his or her agents could cumulatively 
contribute more than $250 to a board or commission member. 
The 
Commission, of course, has the authority to construe the Act 
liberally to accomplish its purposes. 
Section 81003. 
Your second question was whether the principle of 
aggregation applies to both Section 84308(a) and Section 
84308(b). 
Nothing in the regulation indicates that it should De 
limi ted to Section 84308 (b), and we see no policy reason for 
such a narrow interpretation. 
4. 
Applicability to Collective Groups: 
Your last question 
concerned the applicability of Section 84308 to collective 
groups. 
As a general rule, the Commission has not interpreted 
the conflict of interest provisions of the Act to require 
disqualification under Section 87100 when a public official has 
received income from a partner of an individual who will be 
affected by a governmental decision, or from a PAC or other 
m~mbers of a trade association of which such an individual is a 
m~mber. 
We anticipate taking the same general approach under 
Section 84308 to questions of contributions made 
partners of 


Frederick K. Lowell 
February 7, 1983 
Page 4 
an applicant, or by a PAC or other members of a trade asso-
ciation in which the applicant is a member. 
However, I should 
caution you that with respect to this question, as with your 
question concerning agents, there may be a wide range of factual 
situations, some of whicn could possibly trigger the provisions 
of Section 84308. 
Therefore, if you are unsure in a specific 
case wnether or not Section 84308 applies, I would suggest that 
you ask for advice under Section 83114(b). 
I hope this letter has been of assistance to you. 
If you 
have any further questions on Section 84308, please do not 
hesitate to contct me. 
BAM:plh 
Attachments 
I 
Very truly yours,\ 
_ 
! b! ()./7)LfI /, (l (2 ))GL~/tL-Cd~ 
slii~ara A.~an 
Chief 
iLegal Division 


LAW OFFICE.S OF 
PILLSBURY, MADISON & SUTRO 
LONDON 
105 VICTORIA STREET 
LONDON SWIE 6 Qu 
TELEPHONE 01-828~4521 
CABLE ADDRESS"EVANSf! 
TELEX 34743 
TELECOPIER (4IS~ 396-2096 
WRlTER'S DIRECT DIAL NUMBER 
(415) 983-1585 
225 BUSH STREET 
POST OFFICE BOX 7880 
SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94120 
TELEPHONE (415) 983-1000 
Ms. Barbara A. Milman 
Attorney at Law 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
1100 K Street 
Sacramento, CA 95814 
Dear Barbara: 
19 
ENTEENTH STREET, N. W, 
NGTON. D, C. 20036 
ONE (202; 887-0300 
LOS ANGELES 
700 SOUTH FLOWER STREET 
LOS 
CALIFORNIA 90017 
(213; 629-9500 
SAN JOSE 
101 PARK CENTER PLAZA 
SAN JOSE 1 CALIFORNIA 9S113 
TELEPHONE (408) 287-2233 
January 18, 1983 
Scope of A.B. 1040 
Pursuant to Government Code section 83114(b), 
this letter constitutes a request for technical advice 
with respect to the scope of A.B. 1040 which added sec-
tion 84308 to the Government Code effective January 1, 
1983. 
I have the following questions: 
1. 
Definition of "Agent." 
Government Code 
section 84308(a) prohibitS-elected officers, alternates 
and candidates serving on quasi-judicial boards or com-
missions from accepting, soliciting or directing con-
tributions of $250 or more from agents of applicants and 
agents of persons who actively support or oppose appli-
cations. 
Under regulations recently promulgated by the 
Commission, an "agent" is defined as only including a 
person who "represents" a principal in connection with 
the proceeding (2 Cal.Admin.Code, § 18438.3(b». 
A ques-
tion arises as to what "represents" means in this context. 
It seems clear that many applicants may use law, archi-
tectural, engineering or consulting firms in the course 
of applying for a permit before a quasi-judicial board or 
commission. 
Does the Commission take the position that 
the mere provision of technical data which is included 


an applicant's application is sufficient to render the 
data provider an "agent" under the regulation or, does 
a person only become an agent by appearing at a proceed-
ing or engaging in direct communication (as defined in 
the Act) with members of the board? 
The latter interpre-
tation (i.e., requiring direct communication) is consistent 
with the Commission's regulation defining "persons who 
actively support or oppose" (2 Cal.Admin.Code, § 18438.4) 
since such persons are only deemed to be those who communi-
cate directly with members of the board or commission. 
2. 
Definition of "Contributions." 
Under sec-
tion 18438.5(a) of the Commission's regulations, the term 
"contributions" is defined as made for the purpose of in-
fluencing or attempting to influence the action of the 
voters in Federal as well as in state or local elections, 
although presumably only for the purposes of Government 
Code section 84308. 
The regulation by California of con-
tributions to Federal office is preempted by the Federal 
Election Campaign Act of 1971, as amended (2 U.S.C. § 453; 
11 C.F.R. § 108.7). 
Assuming, however, that the Commission 
is required by Article III, section 3.5 of the California 
Constitution to ignore the fact of Federal preemption, is 
it correct to infer from the language of the statute that 
Federal contributions are only covered under section 84308(b) 
and not under section 84308(a)? 
3. 
Aggregation of Contributions. 
Section 
18438.5(b) of the Commission's regulations states that 
in order to determine whether a contribution of $250 or 
more has been made by a person or his or her agent, con-
tributions made by that person within the preceding 12 
months must be aggregated with those made by his or her 
agent within the preceding 12 months or the period of the 
agency relationship, whichever is shorter. 
This regula-
tion appears to conflict with the language in section 
84308(a) which prohibits a restricted person from accept-
ing or soliciting a contribution of $250 or more from an 
applicant or his or her agent. 
Similarly, section 84308(b) 
requires disqualification of a member or board or commis-
sion if he or she has knowingly received a contribution of 
$250 or more within the preceding 12 months from the appli-
cant or his or her agent. 
I note further that it is not 
clear-Whether the aggregation is meant only to apply for 
the purposes of disqualification of a member under section 
84308(b) or whether it is also meant to apply to the pro-
hibition set forth in section 84308(a). 
4. 
Applicability to Collective Groups. 
An 
additional question arises with respect to the applica-
bility of this law to a collective group of persons. 
For 
example, if a member of a trade association is an appli-
cant for a 
and 
associat 
active 
sup-
ports the application, does the limitation on contributions 
2. 


to the members of the board or commission apply to all of 
the other members of the trade or membership association? 
Does the limitation apply to the political action committee 
of the trade or membership association? 
Similarly, in the 
case of a partnership, if one partner is an applicant are 
all the other partners subject to the limitation? 
Please contact me if you have any questions con-
cerning this letter. 
Thank you for your attention on this 
matter. 
(r 
! 
vety tru~y 
V 
/ 
M' 
Frederick K. Lowell

San Francisco, CA 
94120 
Dear Fred: 
Thank you for your letter dated Decembeer 22, 1982, in 
which you asked for confirmation of my previous verbal advice 
regarding attempts to influence the Governor's appointments. 
Your specific question was whether such attempts constitute 
"legislative or administrative action" pursuant to the lobbying 
provisions of the Political Reform Act. 
Because you did not ask 
this question on behalf of a specifically identified client, 
this letter does not constitute advice under Section 83114 of 
the Government Code. 
The process of making appointments to state agencies is 
not defined as "legislative or administrative action" and, 
therefore, attempts to influence the Governor '·s dec is ion on such 
appointments is not reportaole by persons filing statements 
pursuant to Government Code Sections 86107 and 86108. 
You are, however, correct in your assumption that once a 
particular appointment has been made by the Governor, any 
attempts to influence the legislative approval process would 
constitute "legislative action" pursuant to Government Code 
Section 82037 which states in part that: 
"Legislative action" means the drafting, 
introduction, consideration, modification, enactment 
or defeat of any bill, resolution, amendment, report, 
nomination or other matter by the Legislature ••. 
If I can be of any further assistance, please feel free 
to contact me. 
Sincerely, 
~~ri~ 
Lynn Montgomery 
Political Reform Consultant

~ 
105 VICTORIA 5TRE£,.. 
I..ONOON SWIE is OW 
Te:L..£PHON£ OI·82S· ... !5cn 
CA8L..£ .400RESS"eV.4NS" 
T!:.L..£X 34743 
T!!:l..ECO""E'" :.<15) 396-20905 
WRITE""!! OIFlECT OIAl.. NUMe!!:", 
(415) 983-1585 
PIL.LSBURY, MADISON & SUTRO

SAN F'RANCISCO. CAI.IF'ORNfA 94120

n:l..s:,. ... ONs: C20a) 667-0300

700 SOUTJoof F'L"OWEA ST~e:E" 
:..05 ANGEl..£S. CAt..IF'ORNIA. 900!7 
'"El..s:,. ... ONE :213) O5a9-9500 
SA.N , • .'ose: 
~Ol PARK CENTER Pt..AZA 
SAN ,JOSE. CAL"IF"ORNIA 95113 
TEL..£.?HON£ ~40e) ZSi·aZ:J3 
December 22, 1982 
Ms. Lynn Montgomery 
Political Reform Act of 1974 -
Lobbying Activities-
Fair Political Practices Commission 
1100 K Street 
Sacramento, CA 95814 
Dear Lynn: 
Pursuant to our conversation yesterday, this 
letter constitutes a request for technical advice with 
respect to attempts to influence the Governor's appoint-
ments. 
I would appreciate it if you would confirm my 
understanding that attempts to influence Governor-elect 
Deu~~ejian's appointments to state agencies do not con-
stitute attempts to influence legislative or administra-
tive action within the scope of the lobbying disclosure 
provisions of the Political Reform Act of 1974. 
It is 
my understanding that the action of the Governor in making 
appointments to state offices does not constitute "legis-
lative action" because this is not a matter for considera-
tion by the legislature. 
Similarly, I understand that in-
fluencing the Governor's appointments does not constitute 
influencing administrative action because the appointment 
of an agency official does not constitute a quasi-legislative 
or rate-making proceeding of a state agency. 
Notwithstanding the foregoing, I assume that when 
a particular appointment is subject to legislative approval 
and an attempt is made to influence the legislature, then 


such activities would constitute influencing legislative 
action under the Act. 
I enjoyed talking with you yesterday in Sacramento 
and I look forward to your response. ;! 
ve~' 
yo~rs, 
/(~ 
Frederick K. Lowell 
2.

However, 'I will try to answer your questions

You have asKed whether an

2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18438.3(b). 
In general, unless such a

sion, we would not consider the firm to be an agent. 
However, 
when a firm goes beyond "mere provision of technical data" --
for example, when an engineering firm prepares an application" 
including written materials bearing the engineering firm's name 
and advocating the applicant's position -- the firm might be

2. 
Definition of Contributions: 
In your second question 
you asked about 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18438.5(a) , which 
defines contributions for purposes of Section 84308. 
You 
suggested that the inclusion of federal contributions in that 
definition is preempted by 2 USC Section 453. 
In this respect I 
suggest that you look at the Opinion of Counsel of the Federal 
Elections Commission (OC 1975-14) concerning preemption of a 
Massachusetts statute containing provisions similar to Section 
84308. 
I have included a copy of the opinion for your con-
venience. 
The opinion concluded that a number of provisions 
relating to contributions, including restrictions on the 
solicitation and receipt of campaign contributions by appointed 
state officials (Chapter 55, Mass. General Laws, Sections 
11-17), were not preempted by 2 USc Section 453. 
In light of

problem with the definition of contribution in 2 Cal. Adm. Code 
Section 18438.5(a} i therefore there is no need to consider the 
application of Article III, Section 3.5 of the California

Frederick K. Lowell 
February 7, 1983  
You also asked whether the definition in 2 Cal. Adm. Code 
Section 18438.5(a) applies to Doth Section 84308(a) and Section 
84308(0). 
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18438.5 (a) was intended to 
provide a single definition of contribution for Section 84308, 
and thus applies to Secton 84308(a) as well as to Section 
84308 (b) . 
3. 
Aggregation of Contrioutions: 
You raised two questions 
regarding 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18438.5(b). 
First, you point 
out that the statute refers to contributions from an applicant 
or his or her agent, and question whether, in view of this 
language, the statute permits any aggregation of contributions. 
The word "or," while most often used as a disjunctive term, is 
often used in stautes as a conjunctive term. 
See, e.g. De Sylva 
v. Ballentine (1956) 76 S.Ct. 974, 976, 977, 351 US 570, 573, 
575, 1002 Ed 1415. 
In fact, there are a number of other places 
in the Political Reform Act where "or" is used in a conjunctive 
sense. 
E.g. Section 84200(b) 
(" ... in an odd-numbered year, 
have made contributions or independent expenditures of five 
h undr ed dollar s ... ") i Section 87103 (a) 
("any bus iness en ti ty in 
which the public official has a direct or indirect interest 
worth more than one thousand dollars .. :") . 
The Commission has determined that the interpretation in 
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18438.5(b) best carries out the 
purposes of Section 84308. 
It would defeat the purposes of this 
section if an applicant and his or her agents could cumulatively 
contribute more than $250 to a board or commission member. 
The 
Commission, of course, has the authority to construe the Act 
liberally to accomplish its purposes. 
Section 81003. 
Your second question was whether the principle of 
aggregation applies to both Section 84308(a) and Section 
84308(b). 
Nothing in the regulation indicates that it should De 
limi ted to Section 84308 (b), and we see no policy reason for 
such a narrow interpretation. 
4. 
Applicability to Collective Groups: 
Your last question 
concerned the applicability of Section 84308 to collective 
groups. 
As a general rule, the Commission has not interpreted 
the conflict of interest provisions of the Act to require 
disqualification under Section 87100 when a public official has 
received income from a partner of an individual who will be 
affected by a governmental decision, or from a PAC or other 
m~mbers of a trade association of which such an individual is a 
m~mber. 
We anticipate taking the same general approach under 
Section 84308 to questions of contributions made 
partners of 


Frederick K. Lowell 
February 7, 1983  
an applicant, or by a PAC or other members of a trade asso-
ciation in which the applicant is a member. 
However, I should 
caution you that with respect to this question, as with your 
question concerning agents, there may be a wide range of factual 
situations, some of whicn could possibly trigger the provisions 
of Section 84308. 
Therefore, if you are unsure in a specific 
case wnether or not Section 84308 applies, I would suggest that 
you ask for advice under Section 83114(b).
sections.conclusion: General guidance provided on each topic: (1) A firm providing merely technical data would not be considered an agent unless it appears before the board/commission or communicates directly with members; (2) Federal contributions are not preempted under 2 USC Section 453 based on FEC Opinion OC 1975-14; the definition applies to both Section 84308(a) and (b); (3) Aggregation of contributions from an applicant and his/her agents is permitted and applies to both subsections; (4) As a general rule, contributions from partners or PAC members are not triggering disqualification, but specific factual situations may require individual advice.
sections.question_synthetic: The letter addresses four questions regarding the scope of Government Code Section 84308 (AB 1040): the definition of "agent" for purposes of contribution restrictions, whether federal election contributions are included in the definition of contributions, how contributions should be aggregated to determine if the $250 threshold is met, and whether the statute applies to contributions made by partners or members of trade associations of applicants.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC provided general guidance clarifying that: agents must directly communicate with the board/commission (not merely provide technical data); federal contributions are included and not preempted; contributions must be aggregated from both applicants and their agents to determine if the $250 threshold is crossed; and contributions from partners or trade association members generally do not trigger Section 84308 disqualification, though specific cases should seek individual advice.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document contains both the incoming request letter (from Pillsbury, Madison & Sutro dated January 18, 1983) and the FPPC response letter (from Chief Barbara A. Milman dated February 7, 1983). The FPPC response explicitly states it does not constitute formal advice under Section 83114 due to lack of specific client context, though general guidance is provided. OCR quality is good with minor typographical errors (e.g., 'contrioutions' instead of 'contributions').

In light of 
this opinion it does not appear that there is any preemption 
problem with the definition of contribution in 2 Cal. Adm. Code 
Section 18438.5(a} i therefore there is no need to consider the 
application of Article III, Section 3.5 of the California 
Constitution to Section 84308 or to the Commission's regulation. 


Frederick K. Lowell 
February 7, 1983  
You also asked whether the definition in 2 Cal. Adm. Code 
Section 18438.5(a) applies to Doth Section 84308(a) and Section 
84308(0). 
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18438.5 (a) was intended to 
provide a single definition of contribution for Section 84308, 
and thus applies to Secton 84308(a) as well as to Section 
84308 (b) . 
3. 
Aggregation of Contrioutions: 
You raised two questions 
regarding 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18438.5(b). 
First, you point 
out that the statute refers to contributions from an applicant 
or his or her agent, and question whether, in view of this 
language, the statute permits any aggregation of contributions. 
The word "or," while most often used as a disjunctive term, is 
often used in stautes as a conjunctive term. 
See, e.g. De Sylva 
v. Ballentine (1956) 76 S.Ct. 974, 976, 977, 351 US 570, 573, 
575, 1002 Ed 1415. 
In fact, there are a number of other places 
in the Political Reform Act where "or" is used in a conjunctive 
sense. 
E.g. Section 84200(b) 
(" ... in an odd-numbered year, 
have made contributions or independent expenditures of five 
h undr ed dollar s ... ") i Section 87103 (a) 
("any bus iness en ti ty in 
which the public official has a direct or indirect interest 
worth more than one thousand dollars .. :") . 
The Commission has determined that the interpretation in 
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18438.5(b) best carries out the 
purposes of Section 84308. 
It would defeat the purposes of this 
section if an applicant and his or her agents could cumulatively 
contribute more than $250 to a board or commission member. 
The 
Commission, of course, has the authority to construe the Act 
liberally to accomplish its purposes. 
Section 81003. 
Your second question was whether the principle of 
aggregation applies to both Section 84308(a) and Section 
84308(b). 
Nothing in the regulation indicates that it should De 
limi ted to Section 84308 (b), and we see no policy reason for 
such a narrow interpretation. 
4. 
Applicability to Collective Groups: 
Your last question 
concerned the applicability of Section 84308 to collective 
groups. 
As a general rule, the Commission has not interpreted 
the conflict of interest provisions of the Act to require 
disqualification under Section 87100 when a public official has 
received income from a partner of an individual who will be 
affected by a governmental decision, or from a PAC or other 
m~mbers of a trade association of which such an individual is a 
m~mber. 
We anticipate taking the same general approach under 
Section 84308 to questions of contributions made 
partners of 


Frederick K. Lowell 
February 7, 1983  
an applicant, or by a PAC or other members of a trade asso-
ciation in which the applicant is a member. 
However, I should 
caution you that with respect to this question, as with your 
question concerning agents, there may be a wide range of factual 
situations, some of whicn could possibly trigger the provisions 
of Section 84308. 
Therefore, if you are unsure in a specific 
case wnether or not Section 84308 applies, I would suggest that 
you ask for advice under Section 83114(b).

CONCLUSION: General guidance provided on each topic: (1) A firm providing merely technical data would not be considered an agent unless it appears before the board/commission or communicates directly with members; (2) Federal contributions are not preempted under 2 USC Section 453 based on FEC Opinion OC 1975-14; the definition applies to both Section 84308(a) and (b); (3) Aggregation of contributions from an applicant and his/her agents is permitted and applies to both subsections; (4) As a general rule, contributions from partners or PAC members are not triggering disqualification, but specific factual situations may require individual advice.

Lowell Pillsbury, Madison & Sutro 225 Bush Street P.O. Box 7880 San Francisco, CA 94120 Dear Mr. Lowell: February 7, 1983 Re: Our File No. A-83-012 Stateme n ts of Econ om i c Interest 322-6444 In your letter of January 18, 1983, you asked a number of questions concerning the scope of Government Code Section 84308* ("AB 1040") and the Commission's regulations interpreting that section. In particular, you asked about: (1) The definition of "agent" in 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18438.3(b) i (2) The inclusion of contributions to federal election campaigns in the definition of contrioutions in 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18438.5 (a) i (3) The provision for aggregation of contrioutions in 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18438.5(b) i (4) The applicability of Section 84308 to a situation in which a partner of an individual, or a PAC or other memOers of a trade association to which an individual oelongs, make contributions to memoers of a quasi-judicial board or commission before which tne individual has an application pending. Because you did not aSK these questions on behalf of specifically identified clients, and did not provide a I *All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise noted. Frederick K. Lowell February 7, 1983 Page 2 .pecific factual context for the questions, this letter does not constitute advice under Section 83114. See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18329 (b). However, 'I will try to answer your questions in general terms in order to clarify the Commission's intent in adopting its regulations, and to point out areas in which you may wish to make requests for more specific advice under Section 83114 on behalf of your clients. 1. Definition of Agent: You have asKed whether an architectural, engineering or law firm that "merely" provides technical data which is included in an application is the applicant's "agent" within the meaning of Section 84308 and 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18438.3(b). In general, unless such a firm eitner appears before tne board or commission in person, or communicates directly with the members of the board or commis- sion, we would not consider the firm to be an agent. However, when a firm goes beyond "mere provision of technical data" -- for example, when an engineering firm prepares an application" including written materials bearing the engineering firm's name and advocating the applicant's position -- the firm might be considered to be "directly communicating" with the board or commission and thus to be an "agent" of the applicant. Clearly, there will be many gradations of involvement by such firms in applications to agencies covered by Section 84308. When you are faced with a specific situation for a specific client, we will be glad to provide you with more specific advice. 2. Definition of Contributions: In your second question you asked about 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18438.5(a)
embedding.summary: This is an FPPC advice letter responding to general questions about the scope and application of Government Code Section 84308 (AB 1040), which restricts contributions to members of quasi-judicial boards and commissions. The FPPC provided interpretive guidance on four key regulatory issues while noting that because the questions lacked specific client facts, the letter does not constitute formal advice under Section 83114.
